_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for York County, VA. Record survival for one of Virginia's best-documented colonial counties — records survive from the 1630s, among the earliest continuous county records in English America.

previous_courthouses.location: Yorktown — early courts met in various locations before a permanent courthouse was built
previous_courthouses.fate: Early structures replaced. Records survived transfers.

previous_courthouses.location: Yorktown — brick courthouse on the main street
previous_courthouses.fate: Damaged during the 1781 siege (British used it during the battle). Repaired and used through the Civil War. Damaged again during the 1862 siege. Records survived both sieges.

record_losses.description: The courthouse was within the British defensive perimeter during the 1781 siege and was damaged by bombardment. However, the bound record volumes survived the siege remarkably intact. York County's records from 1633 onward are among the most complete in colonial Virginia.
record_losses.narrative_impact: York County's records survived two sieges — 1781 and 1862 — making them one of the longest continuous county record series in English America. If {ancestor_name} lived in York County, the paper trail stretches back further than almost anywhere else.
record_losses.source: Ketchum, Richard M. Victory at Yorktown. New York: Henry Holt, 2004.

record_losses.cause: Second Siege of Yorktown — Peninsula Campaign
records_destroyed: scattered justice of the peace records
record_losses.description: McClellan's army besieged Yorktown in April-May 1862. The courthouse was again in the combat zone but the bound record books survived. Confederate officials may have removed some records before the siege; most were returned.
record_losses.narrative_impact: Two sieges in 81 years, and the records survived both. {ancestor_name}'s deed or will from colonial York County has better odds of survival than in almost any other Tidewater county.
record_losses.source: Sears, Stephen W. To the Gates of Richmond. Boston: Ticknor & Fields, 1992.

deeds.completeness: Excellent — among the earliest and most complete county deed records in Virginia. Continuous series from 1633.
deeds.gaps: Minor gaps in some volumes. Overall completeness is exceptional.
deeds.repository: York County Clerk of Court, Yorktown; Library of Virginia, Richmond; Colonial National Historical Park archives
deeds.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; Ancestry.com VA land records; Library of Virginia digital collections. Published abstracts available.

wills_estates.completeness: Excellent — will books survive from 1633. Estate inventories are particularly rich for the colonial period.
wills_estates.gaps: Minimal. Some loose estate papers from later periods are incomplete.
wills_estates.repository: York County Clerk of Court; Library of Virginia
wills_estates.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; Library of Virginia digital collections; published abstracts

marriages.completeness: Marriage bonds from 1772. Earlier marriages documented in York-Hampton Parish register and court order entries.
marriages.gaps: No formal marriage bonds before 1772. Parish registers fill the gap for the colonial period.
marriages.repository: York County Clerk of Court; Library of Virginia

court_minutes.completeness: Excellent — one of the most complete colonial court record series in Virginia. Order books run nearly continuously from 1633.
court_minutes.gaps: Minor gaps in some volumes. Content quality is high — court minutes include detailed personal and economic information.
court_minutes.repository: Library of Virginia; York County Clerk of Court; Colonial Williamsburg Foundation research library
court_minutes.online_access: Library of Virginia microfilm; published transcriptions available

vital_records.completeness: Virginia state registration began 1912. Earlier vital events in York-Hampton Parish records.
vital_records.repository: Virginia Department of Health, Office of Vital Records

church_records.completeness: York-Hampton Parish vestry book and register fragments survive from the mid-17th century. Among the richest colonial parish records in Virginia.
church_records.repository: Library of Virginia; York County Clerk of Court; Grace Episcopal Church, Yorktown

substitute_sources.description: The vestry book and register of York-Hampton Parish survive from the mid-17th century, providing baptism, marriage, burial, tithable, and processioning records that supplement the exceptionally complete civil record.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: York-Hampton Parish records are among the richest colonial church records in Virginia. Combined with the county court records from 1633, they make York County one of the best-documented colonial jurisdictions in America.
substitute_sources.source: Goodwin, W. A. R. Historical Sketch of Bruton Church, Williamsburg, Virginia. Petersburg, 1903.

substitute_sources.description: York County's colonial records have been extensively published and abstracted. Multiple volumes of deed, will, and court order abstracts are available in print.
substitute_sources.repository: Available in major genealogical libraries; FamilySearch catalog; Ancestry.com book collections
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Published abstracts provide convenient access to the colonial records without visiting the courthouse. They also serve as finding aids to the originals.
substitute_sources.source: Fleet, Beverly. Virginia Colonial Abstracts. Various volumes. Richmond, 1937–1949.

substitute_sources.description: Population schedules 1790–1940. York County is a small county with manageable census records.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Census records complement the exceptionally complete court records. Agricultural and slave schedules (1850–1860) add economic detail.
substitute_sources.source: National Archives and Records Administration. Federal Census Schedules, York County, VA.

narrative_hooks: York County's records begin in 1633 — three years before Harvard was founded, 143 years before the Declaration of Independence — and if {ancestor_name} lived here in the colonial period, the odds of finding {pronoun_possessive} name in those records are better than in almost any other county in America.

The courthouse at Yorktown survived two sieges — 1781 and 1862 — and {ancestor_name}'s deed or will survived with it, a document that outlasted the battles that ended both the Revolution and the first campaign of the Civil War.

York-Hampton Parish's register may record {ancestor_name}'s baptism, marriage, or burial from the 1600s — one of the few places in America where church records reach back to the early colonial period.

York County's colonial court minutes are so detailed that {ancestor_name}'s dispute with a neighbor, purchase of a cow, or appointment as a road overseer may appear in the record — these are not just land and probate records but a running narrative of daily life.
source_references: Ketchum, Richard M. Victory at Yorktown: The Campaign That Won the Revolution. New York: Henry Holt, 2004.
Sears, Stephen W. To the Gates of Richmond: The Peninsula Campaign. Boston: Ticknor & Fields, 1992.
Fleet, Beverly. Virginia Colonial Abstracts. Various volumes. Richmond, 1937–1949.
Library of Virginia. A Guide to Virginia County and City Records on Microfilm. Richmond, 2001.
Goodwin, W. A. R. Historical Sketch of Bruton Church, Williamsburg, Virginia. Petersburg, 1903.
